JPEG files seem to possess a self-synchronization property, which allows the decoder to eventually get the same decoding sequence as the original even if bit errors occur in the bitstream.
Standard JPEG decoders lack error-resilient techniques, which prevent them from utilizing the self-synchronization property observed in JPEG files.
JPEG decoders lack error-resilient techniques, prompting the need for an error-resilient mechanism.
A decoding failure results in an exception being reported to abort the remaining blocks’ decoding.
The error-resilient mechanism is proposed in the robust decoder shown in Fig. 4.
The decoder encounters invalid codewords during Huffman decoding in an MCU block.
Invalid codewords encountered by the decoder cannot be found in the Huffman tables of the JPEG header.
If the number of decoded coefficients in an 8×8 block exceeds 64, it leads to coefficients overflow.
Once a decoding failure is detected, the error-resilient mechanism discards bits to continue processing.
